name: Handle Sensitive Variables Securely
description: Manage secrets, API keys, and passwords in Terraform without exposing them in state, logs, or version control.

steps.title: Mark variables as sensitive
steps.description: Use the sensitive flag to prevent Terraform from displaying variable values in plan output and logs.
steps.tip: Sensitive variables show as '(sensitive value)' in plan output, preventing accidental exposure.

steps.title: Pass secrets via environment variables
steps.description: Use TF_VAR_ prefixed environment variables to inject secrets without putting them in files.
steps.command:
export TF_VAR_db_password="my-secret-password"
export TF_VAR_api_key="sk-abc123"
terraform plan
steps.warning: Never put secrets in .tfvars files that are committed to version control. Use environment variables or a secrets manager.

steps.title: Use a secrets manager data source
steps.description: Fetch secrets at plan time from AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, or similar services.

steps.title: Encrypt state at rest
steps.description: Ensure your remote backend encrypts state files since they may contain sensitive values.
steps.warning: State files can contain secrets in plaintext even when variables are marked sensitive. Always encrypt state at rest.

steps.title: Add .gitignore rules for sensitive files
steps.description: Prevent accidental commits of files that may contain secrets.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Terraform Secret Management Setup
# Configures secure variable handling

echo "==> Checking for secrets in tracked files..."
if git ls-files | xargs grep -l 'password|secret|api_key|token' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '.gitignore' | grep -v 'variables.tf'; then
  echo "WARNING: Potential secrets found in tracked files!"
  echo "Review the files listed above."
else
  echo "No obvious secrets found in tracked files."
fi

echo ""
echo "==> Checking .gitignore..."
for PATTERN in "*.tfstate" "*.tfvars" ".terraform/"; do
  if grep -q "$PATTERN" .gitignore 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "  OK: $PATTERN is in .gitignore"
  else
    echo "  MISSING: $PATTERN not in .gitignore"
    echo "$PATTERN" >> .gitignore
    echo "    -> Added to .gitignore"
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "==> Checking for sensitive variables without the sensitive flag..."
grep -rn 'variable.*password|variable.*secret|variable.*key|variable.*token' *.tf 2>/dev/null | while read -r LINE; do
  echo "  Review: $LINE"
done

echo ""
echo "==> Secret management checklist:"
echo "  [ ] All secrets use 'sensitive = true' in variable blocks"
echo "  [ ] Secrets passed via TF_VAR_ env vars or secrets manager"
echo "  [ ] State backend has encrypt = true"
echo "  [ ] .gitignore covers .tfstate and .tfvars files"
echo "  [ ] No secrets hardcoded in .tf files"

faq.question: Does marking a variable as sensitive protect it in state?
faq.answer: No. The sensitive flag only hides values from CLI output and logs. Secrets can still appear in the state file. Always encrypt your state backend and restrict access to state files.

faq.question: Should I use Vault or AWS Secrets Manager with Terraform?
faq.answer: Yes, for production workloads. Data sources fetch secrets at plan time without storing them in your config. HashiCorp Vault and AWS Secrets Manager both have Terraform provider support.
